original_hypothesis.title: Locking Outside Active Lockdrop Period Zeroes Stored Remainder
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In _lock, the local variable remainder is initialized to 0. The block that computes the new remainder only executes during an active lockdrop. Outside the lockdrop period, remainder stays 0. At the end of _lock, lockedToken.remainder is unconditionally overwritten with this local variable, destroying any previously accumulated remainder from a lockdrop.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The unconditional assignment lockedToken.remainder = remainder at line 343 overwrites the stored value. When the lockdrop block (lines 324-335) is skipped, remainder = 0, destroying any prior value. This is a state corruption bug.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 remainder; (defaults to 0) at line 315; conditional NFT calculation at lines 324-335; unconditional lockedToken.remainder = remainder at line 343

preconditions: User accumulated a non-zero remainder from a previous lockdrop lock
User locks additional tokens outside an active lockdrop period

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Local variable defaults to 0; conditional assignment only during lockdrop; unconditional storage write
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: lockedToken.remainder silently zeroed for any lock outside lockdrop
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should preserve existing remainder when lockdrop not active, or scope the assignment inside the lockdrop conditional

analysis_questions: What is the typical remainder value in economic terms?
Can users avoid this by not locking outside lockdrop?
Does this interact with the phantom remainder bug (TECH-H005)?
validation_requirements: Lock during lockdrop to create remainder
Lock additional tokens after lockdrop ends

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: User locks 150 tokens during lockdrop (nftCost=100). Gets 1 NFT, remainder=50. Lockdrop ends. User locks 60 more tokens. Outside lockdrop, no NFT calc runs. lockedToken.remainder = 0 (local default). User loses 50 tokens of NFT progress permanently.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of partial NFT credit (remainder value); up to nftCost-1 tokens worth per user per token
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user who locks outside lockdrop with existing remainder
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Permanent loss — remainder cannot be recovered

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for locking outside lockdrop period with pre-existing remainder

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing remainder before/after lock outside lockdrop

validation_result.impact_summary: Any lock operation outside an active lockdrop unconditionally zeroes the user's stored remainder, permanently destroying partial-NFT credit accumulated during a previous lockdrop.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The _lock function declares a local variable `uint256 remainder;` which defaults to 0. The only code path that assigns a non-zero value to this local variable is inside the lockdrop-active conditional block. Outside the lockdrop period, this block is skipped and `remainder` stays 0. At line 343, the assignment `lockedToken.remainder = remainder;` is unconditional, overwriting the storage value with 0. Notably, the old remainder IS loaded into the local `quantity` variable at `uint256 quantity = _quantity + lockedToken.remainder;`, but this local `quantity` variable is never used outside the lockdrop block—it only feeds the NFT calculation which is also inside the lockdrop conditional. So the old remainder is effectively read, added to a throwaway computation, and then the storage is zeroed.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof-of-signal is accurate. The local `remainder` variable is declared without initialization (defaults to 0), is only assigned inside the lockdrop conditional, and `lockedToken.remainder = remainder` is written unconditionally. The exact lines match the claimed pattern in LockManager.sol.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The local variable `remainder` defaults to 0. It is only set to a non-zero value inside the lockdrop-active block. When a user locks tokens outside a lockdrop, the lockdrop block is skipped, `remainder` stays 0, and the unconditional `lockedToken.remainder = remainder` zeroes out any previously stored remainder.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls lock() or lockOnBehalf() which routes to _lock()
2. _lock reads lockedToken.remainder into local `quantity`: quantity = _quantity + lockedToken.remainder (line ~315)
3. _lock declares local `uint256 remainder;` which is 0 by default (line ~316)
4. Lockdrop condition check: lockdrop.start <= block.timestamp && lockdrop.end >= block.timestamp (line ~322-324)
5. If lockdrop NOT active: the entire if-block (lines 324-335) is skipped, `remainder` stays 0
6. Unconditional storage write: lockedToken.remainder = remainder (line 343) - writes 0, destroying old value
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When block.timestamp is outside the [lockdrop.start, lockdrop.end] window, the if-block at lines 322-335 is skipped entirely. The local `remainder` variable retains its default value of 0. At line 343, `lockedToken.remainder = remainder` overwrites whatever was previously stored (e.g., 50 tokens of NFT progress) with 0. The old remainder was added to the local `quantity` variable at line 315, but `quantity` is never used outside the lockdrop block, so this loaded value is simply discarded.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No config dependency. The bug is in the core logic of _lock and occurs regardless of configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario: (1) During lockdrop with nftCost=100e18, user locks 150e18 tokens. NFT calc: remainder = 150e18 % 100e18 = 50e18, numberNFTs = 1. lockedToken.remainder = 50e18. (2) Lockdrop ends. User locks 10e18 more tokens. In _lock: quantity = 10e18 + 50e18 = 60e18 (local, unused). Local `remainder` = 0 (lockdrop block skipped). lockedToken.remainder = 0. User permanently loses 50e18 tokens worth of NFT progress. (3) Next lockdrop starts. User locks 60e18 tokens. quantity = 60e18 + 0 = 60e18. remainder = 60e18 % 100e18 = 60e18. No NFTs minted. Had remainder been preserved, quantity would have been 110e18, yielding 1 NFT with remainder 10e18.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Any user who accumulated a non-zero remainder during a lockdrop and subsequently locks additional tokens outside a lockdrop period will permanently lose their accumulated partial-NFT credit. The maximum loss per user per token is nftCost - 1 tokens worth of NFT progress. This silently destroys value without any user-visible error or warning. Users who are active lockers (adding to their positions between lockdrops) are most affected.
recommended_mitigations: Move `lockedToken.remainder = remainder;` inside the lockdrop-active conditional block so remainder is only updated when a new NFT calculation is performed
Alternatively, initialize `remainder` to `lockedToken.remainder` instead of 0: `uint256 remainder = lockedToken.remainder;` so that outside lockdrop, the existing value is preserved
Add a test case that locks during lockdrop, then locks again outside lockdrop, and verifies remainder is preserved
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LockManager with configuredToken (nftCost=100e18). 2. Configure lockdrop with start=now, end=now+1day. 3. User locks 150e18 tokens during lockdrop. 4. Verify lockedToken.remainder == 50e18. 5. Advance time past lockdrop end. 6. User locks 10e18 more tokens. 7. Assert lockedToken.remainder == 50e18 (FAILS - it's 0). 8. This confirms the bug.
uncertainties: The practical economic impact depends on nftCost values and how frequently users lock outside lockdrops
It's unclear whether the protocol intends remainder to carry over between lockdrop periods or be reset
additional_testing_needed: Test locking during lockdrop followed by locking outside lockdrop and checking remainder
Test interaction with the phantom remainder bug (TECH-H005) where remainder persists after full unlock
Test the migration manager path (msg.sender == migrationManager) which also never sets remainder
